expanded_description: A comprehensive exploration of ionic bonding, examining how atoms transfer electrons to form charged ions that attract electrostatically, creating ionic compounds with distinctive properties including high melting points, crystal lattice structures, and electrical conductivity when dissolved or molten.

core_concept.principle: Ionic bonds form when electrons are transferred from metal atoms to nonmetal atoms, creating oppositely charged ions that attract each other through electrostatic forces.
core_concept.explanation: Ionic bonding occurs when atoms with very different electronegativities interact: metals (low electronegativity) lose valence electrons to form positively charged cations, while nonmetals (high electronegativity) gain those electrons to form negatively charged anions. The resulting opposite charges create strong electrostatic attraction (Coulombic force) that holds the ions together in a crystal lattice structure. This electron transfer allows both atoms to achieve stable noble gas electron configurations, satisfying the octet rule. The strength of ionic bonds depends on ion charges and sizes: higher charges and smaller ions create stronger attractions.

image.alt: Diagram showing electron transfer from sodium to chlorine forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions
image.caption: Ionic bonding: electron transfer creates cations and anions that attract electrostatically.

sections.section_title: The Ionic Bonding Process: Electron Transfer
sections.introduction: Ionic bonding begins with the complete transfer of one or more electrons from a metal atom to a nonmetal atom. This transfer is driven by the tendency of both atoms to achieve stable noble gas electron configurations through the octet rule.

key_concept.definition: Ionic bonding is the electrostatic attraction between oppositely charged ions formed by complete electron transfer from metal atoms (forming cations) to nonmetal atoms (forming anions).
key_concept.context: This type of bonding typically occurs between elements with electronegativity differences greater than 1.7, most commonly between Group 1-2 metals and Group 16-17 nonmetals.

principles.statement: Metal atoms lose valence electrons to achieve noble gas configuration, becoming positively charged cations; nonmetal atoms gain those electrons to achieve noble gas configuration, becoming negatively charged anions.
principles.application: Predicts ion charges from group number: Group 1 → +1, Group 2 → +2, Group 16 → -2, Group 17 → -1.
principles.example: Sodium (Na, 3s¹) loses 1 electron → Na⁺ ([Ne]); Chlorine (Cl, 3s²3p⁵) gains 1 electron → Cl⁻ ([Ar]).
principles.analogy: Like passing a ball: the metal 'throws' electrons to the nonmetal, which 'catches' them; both end up with stable 'teams' (electron configurations).

image.alt: Electron transfer from sodium atom to chlorine atom
image.caption: Electron transfer: Na loses 1 electron (becomes Na⁺), Cl gains 1 electron (becomes Cl⁻).

principles.statement: The force of attraction between ions follows Coulomb's Law: $F \propto \dfrac{q_1 q_2}{r^2}$, where q₁ and q₂ are ion charges and r is the distance between ion centers.
principles.application: Explains why MgO (Mg²⁺O²⁻) has higher melting point than NaCl (Na⁺Cl⁻): higher charges create stronger attraction.
principles.example: NaCl: charges +1 and -1, distance 283 pm, lattice energy 787 kJ/mol. MgO: charges +2 and -2, distance 212 pm, lattice energy 3795 kJ/mol.
principles.analogy: Like magnets: stronger magnets (higher charges) or closer distance creates stronger attraction that's harder to break.

principles.principle: Energy Changes in Ionic Bond Formation
principles.statement: Ionic compound formation involves multiple energy changes: ionization energy (endothermic, cost), electron affinity (exothermic, release), and lattice energy (exothermic, large release).
principles.application: Explains why ionic compounds form despite ionization energy cost: lattice energy release more than compensates.
principles.example: NaCl formation: Na ionization (+496 kJ/mol) + Cl electron affinity (-349 kJ/mol) + lattice energy (-787 kJ/mol) = net -640 kJ/mol (exothermic, favorable).

image.alt: Energy diagram for ionic compound formation
image.caption: Ionic formation energetics: lattice energy release exceeds ionization energy cost, making formation favorable.

components.definition: Ionic bond formation requires energy input for ionization but releases more energy through lattice formation.
components.interpretation: Negative ΔH = exothermic (favorable); positive ΔH = endothermic (unfavorable). Lattice energy typically dominates, making ionic compound formation exothermic.

worked_examples.title: Calculating Energy Change in NaCl Formation
worked_examples.problem: Calculate the net energy change when sodium and chlorine form NaCl. Given: Na ionization energy = +496 kJ/mol, Cl electron affinity = -349 kJ/mol, NaCl lattice energy = -787 kJ/mol.

step_by_step.explanation: Identify the energy components for ionic compound formation.
step_by_step.calculation: IE (Na) = +496 kJ/mol (energy to remove electron from Na)

step_by_step.explanation: Add electron affinity energy (energy released when Cl gains electron).
step_by_step.calculation: EA (Cl) = -349 kJ/mol (energy released)

step_by_step.explanation: Add lattice energy (energy released when ions form crystal).
step_by_step.calculation: Lattice Energy = -787 kJ/mol (energy released)

step_by_step.explanation: Sum all energy changes for net formation energy.

worked_examples.final_answer: Net energy change = -640 kJ/mol (exothermic). Despite requiring +496 kJ/mol to ionize sodium, the combined energy release from electron affinity (-349 kJ/mol) and lattice formation (-787 kJ/mol) makes NaCl formation highly favorable. This demonstrates why ionic compounds form spontaneously.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Energy accounting in ionic compound formation

sections.section_title: Formation of Cations: Metal Atoms Losing Electrons
sections.introduction: Metal atoms form positively charged cations by losing valence electrons. This process requires energy (ionization energy) but enables metals to achieve stable noble gas electron configurations.

key_concept.definition: A cation is a positively charged ion formed when a metal atom loses one or more valence electrons, resulting in more protons than electrons.
key_concept.context: Cation formation is endothermic (requires energy input), but the energy cost is compensated by the lattice energy released when ionic compounds form.

classifications.value: Form +1 cations by losing one valence electron (ns¹)
classifications.behavior: Lose single s-electron easily to achieve noble gas configuration; reactivity increases down the group as ionization energy decreases.

examples.explanation: Na (1s²2s²2p⁶3s¹) → Na⁺ (1s²2s²2p⁶) + e⁻. Loses 3s¹ electron to achieve [Ne] configuration. Ionic radius: 102 pm (vs. 186 pm atomic radius).

image.alt: Sodium atom losing electron to form Na⁺
image.caption: Na⁺ formation: loses 3s¹ electron, achieves [Ne] configuration, radius decreases 45%.

examples.explanation: K (4s¹) → K⁺ ([Ar]) + e⁻. Lower ionization energy than Na (419 vs. 496 kJ/mol), making K more reactive.

classifications.value: Form +2 cations by losing two valence electrons (ns²)
classifications.behavior: Lose both s-electrons to achieve noble gas configuration; second ionization energy higher than first but still favorable in compound formation.

examples.explanation: Mg (1s²2s²2p⁶3s²) → Mg²⁺ (1s²2s²2p⁶) + 2e⁻. Loses both 3s electrons to achieve [Ne] configuration. IE₁ = 738 kJ/mol, IE₂ = 1451 kJ/mol.

image.alt: Magnesium atom losing two electrons to form Mg²⁺
image.caption: Mg²⁺ formation: loses both 3s² electrons, achieves [Ne] configuration.

examples.explanation: Ca (4s²) → Ca²⁺ ([Ar]) + 2e⁻. Forms CaO, CaCl₂, etc. Important in biological systems (bones, signaling).

classifications.value: Form variable charge cations by losing s and sometimes d electrons
classifications.behavior: Lose ns electrons first, then (n-1)d electrons; multiple stable oxidation states common due to similar energies of s and d orbitals.

examples.explanation: Fe ([Ar]4s²3d⁶) → Fe²⁺ ([Ar]3d⁶) + 2e⁻ or Fe³⁺ ([Ar]3d⁵) + 3e⁻. Fe³⁺ more stable due to half-filled d⁵ configuration.

image.alt: Iron forming Fe²⁺ and Fe³⁺ ions
image.caption: Transition metals form multiple cations: Fe²⁺ and Fe³⁺ both common.

examples.explanation: Cu ([Ar]4s¹3d¹⁰) → Cu⁺ ([Ar]3d¹⁰) or Cu²⁺ ([Ar]3d⁹). Cu²⁺ more common in aqueous solutions despite Cu⁺ having filled d¹⁰.

principles.statement: Cations are significantly smaller than their parent atoms because electron loss reduces electron-electron repulsion and may remove entire electron shells.
principles.application: Explains why Na⁺ (102 pm) is 45% smaller than Na atom (186 pm); why ionic radii affect lattice energy and solubility.
principles.example: Na: 186 pm → Na⁺: 102 pm (45% reduction). Mg: 160 pm → Mg²⁺: 72 pm (55% reduction). Greater charge = greater size reduction.
principles.analogy: Like deflating a balloon: removing air (electrons) reduces the size; removing more air (higher charge) reduces size more.

principles.statement: Ionization energy increases across periods (harder to remove electrons) and decreases down groups (easier to remove electrons), affecting cation formation tendency.
principles.application: Explains why Cs forms cations more readily than Li; why Al requires more energy to form Al³⁺ than Na requires for Na⁺.
principles.example: Group 1 IE₁: Li (520) > Na (496) > K (419) > Rb (403) > Cs (376) kJ/mol. Lower IE = easier cation formation = more reactive metal.

image.alt: Graph showing ionization energy decreasing down Group 1
image.caption: Ionization energy decreases down groups, making cation formation easier for heavier alkali metals.

worked_examples.title: Predicting Cation Charges from Electron Configuration
worked_examples.problem: Predict the charges of cations formed by: (a) aluminum, (b) calcium, (c) iron. Write electron configurations for each cation.

step_by_step.explanation: Determine valence electrons from group number and electron configuration.
step_by_step.calculation: (a) Al: Group 13, [Ne]3s²3p¹, 3 valence e⁻; (b) Ca: Group 2, [Ar]4s², 2 valence e⁻; (c) Fe: Group 8, [Ar]4s²3d⁶, variable

step_by_step.explanation: Determine electrons to lose for noble gas configuration.
step_by_step.calculation: (a) Al: lose 3 e⁻ → Al³⁺ ([Ne]); (b) Ca: lose 2 e⁻ → Ca²⁺ ([Ar]); (c) Fe: lose 2 e⁻ → Fe²⁺ or 3 e⁻ → Fe³⁺

step_by_step.explanation: Write electron configurations for cations.
step_by_step.calculation: (a) Al³⁺: 1s²2s²2p⁶; (b) Ca²⁺: 1s²2s²2p⁶3s²3p⁶; (c) Fe²⁺: [Ar]3d⁶, Fe³⁺: [Ar]3d⁵

step_by_step.explanation: Verify noble gas configurations for main group elements.
step_by_step.calculation: Al³⁺ = [Ne] ✓; Ca²⁺ = [Ar] ✓; Fe ions don't achieve noble gas config (transition metal)

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) Aluminum forms Al³⁺: [Ne] = 1s²2s²2p⁶. (b) Calcium forms Ca²⁺: [Ar] = 1s²2s²2p⁶3s²3p⁶. (c) Iron forms Fe²⁺: [Ar]3d⁶ or Fe³⁺: [Ar]3d⁵. Main group metals achieve noble gas configurations; transition metals have variable charges based on d-orbital stability.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Predicting cation charges from electron configuration and group position

sections.section_title: Formation of Anions: Nonmetal Atoms Gaining Electrons
sections.introduction: Nonmetal atoms form negatively charged anions by gaining valence electrons. This process typically releases energy (electron affinity) and enables nonmetals to achieve stable noble gas electron configurations.

key_concept.definition: An anion is a negatively charged ion formed when a nonmetal atom gains one or more valence electrons, resulting in more electrons than protons.
key_concept.context: Anion formation is typically exothermic (releases energy) for nonmetals, especially halogens and oxygen group elements, making the process energetically favorable.

classifications.value: Form -1 anions by gaining one electron (ns²np⁵ → ns²np⁶)
classifications.behavior: Gain single electron to complete p sublevel and achieve noble gas configuration; electron affinity becomes less negative down the group (except F < Cl).

examples.explanation: Cl (1s²2s²2p⁶3s²3p⁵) + e⁻ → Cl⁻ (1s²2s²2p⁶3s²3p⁶). Gains 1 electron to achieve [Ar] configuration. EA = -349 kJ/mol (energy released).

image.alt: Chlorine atom gaining electron to form Cl⁻
image.caption: Cl⁻ formation: gains 1 electron to complete 3p sublevel, achieves [Ar] configuration.

examples.explanation: F (2s²2p⁵) + e⁻ → F⁻ (2s²2p⁶). Highest electronegativity (3.98) makes F most eager to gain electrons. EA = -328 kJ/mol.

classifications.value: Form -2 anions by gaining two electrons (ns²np⁴ → ns²np⁶)
classifications.behavior: Gain two electrons to complete p sublevel; first EA exothermic, second EA endothermic (adding e⁻ to negative ion), but lattice energy compensates.

examples.explanation: O (1s²2s²2p⁴) + 2e⁻ → O²⁻ (1s²2s²2p⁶). EA₁ = -141 kJ/mol (exothermic), EA₂ = +744 kJ/mol (endothermic). Net cost offset by lattice energy in compounds like MgO.

image.alt: Oxygen atom gaining two electrons to form O²⁻
image.caption: O²⁻ formation: gains 2 electrons to complete 2p sublevel, achieves [Ne] configuration.

examples.explanation: S (3s²3p⁴) + 2e⁻ → S²⁻ (3s²3p⁶). Forms in metal sulfides like FeS, ZnS. Larger size than O²⁻ (184 pm vs. 140 pm).

classifications.value: Form -3 anions by gaining three electrons (ns²np³ → ns²np⁶)
characteristics: Form -3 ions with very electropositive metals
classifications.behavior: Gain three electrons to complete octet; energetically unfavorable without very electropositive metals; more commonly form covalent bonds.

examples.explanation: N (2s²2p³) + 3e⁻ → N³⁻ (2s²2p⁶). Only forms with very electropositive metals like Li (Li₃N). High energy cost limits occurrence.

image.alt: Nitrogen atom gaining three electrons to form N³⁻
image.caption: N³⁻ formation: gains 3 electrons but only stable with very electropositive metals.

examples.explanation: P (3s²3p³) + 3e⁻ → P³⁻ (3s²3p⁶). Forms in compounds like Na₃P, Ca₃P₂. Larger and more stable than N³⁻ due to larger size.

principles.statement: Anions are significantly larger than their parent atoms because electron gain increases electron-electron repulsion, expanding the electron cloud.
principles.application: Explains why Cl⁻ (181 pm) is 83% larger than Cl atom (99 pm); why ionic radii affect lattice energy and solubility.
principles.example: Cl: 99 pm → Cl⁻: 181 pm (83% increase). O: 66 pm → O²⁻: 140 pm (112% increase). More negative charge = greater expansion.
principles.analogy: Like adding people to a room: more people (electrons) in the same space push against each other, requiring more room (larger radius).

image.alt: Size comparison of atoms and their anions
image.caption: Anions are larger than parent atoms: electron gain increases repulsion, expanding electron cloud.

principles.statement: Electron affinity becomes more negative across periods (easier to add electrons) and less negative down groups (harder to add electrons), with Period 2 exceptions.
principles.application: Explains why Cl has more negative EA than F (less repulsion in larger 3p orbital); why halogens readily form anions.
principles.example: Group 17 EA: F (-328) < Cl (-349) > Br (-325) > I (-295) kJ/mol. Cl most negative due to optimal size (less repulsion than F, closer nucleus than Br/I).

image.alt: Graph showing electron affinity trends in Group 17
image.caption: Electron affinity trend: Cl has most negative EA in Group 17 due to optimal balance of nuclear attraction and electron repulsion.

worked_examples.title: Calculating Anion Formation Energy
worked_examples.problem: Calculate the energy change when chlorine forms Cl⁻ and when oxygen forms O²⁻. Given: Cl EA = -349 kJ/mol; O EA₁ = -141 kJ/mol, EA₂ = +744 kJ/mol.

step_by_step.explanation: Calculate energy for Cl⁻ formation (single electron gain).
step_by_step.calculation: Cl + e⁻ → Cl⁻; ΔH = EA = -349 kJ/mol (exothermic, favorable)

step_by_step.explanation: Calculate energy for O²⁻ formation (two electron gains).
step_by_step.calculation: O + e⁻ → O⁻: EA₁ = -141 kJ/mol; O⁻ + e⁻ → O²⁻: EA₂ = +744 kJ/mol

step_by_step.explanation: Sum the energies for O²⁻ formation.
step_by_step.calculation: Total for O²⁻: -141 + 744 = +603 kJ/mol (endothermic, unfavorable alone)

step_by_step.explanation: Explain why O²⁻ forms despite endothermic EA.
step_by_step.calculation: Lattice energy in compounds like MgO (-3795 kJ/mol) more than compensates for +603 kJ/mol EA cost

worked_examples.final_answer: Cl⁻ formation: -349 kJ/mol (exothermic, spontaneous). O²⁻ formation: +603 kJ/mol (endothermic, requires lattice energy compensation). Chloride forms readily from electron affinity alone; oxide requires the large lattice energy release from ionic compound formation to make the overall process favorable.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Electron affinity calculations for anion formation energetics

sections.section_title: Ionic Compound Formation and Crystal Lattices
sections.introduction: When cations and anions form, they don't exist as isolated ion pairs but arrange themselves in three-dimensional crystal lattices that maximize attractive forces and minimize repulsive forces between ions.

key_concept.definition: An ionic crystal lattice is a regular, repeating three-dimensional arrangement of cations and anions held together by electrostatic forces, where each ion is surrounded by ions of opposite charge.
key_concept.context: The lattice structure maximizes stability by maximizing cation-anion attractions while minimizing anion-anion and cation-cation repulsions through optimal geometric arrangement.

classifications.behavior: Adopted by compounds with similar cation:anion size ratios (0.414-0.732); includes NaCl, KCl, MgO, CaO.

examples.explanation: Na⁺ and Cl⁻ alternate in 3D cubic pattern. Each ion has 6 nearest neighbors of opposite charge. Coordination number = 6 for both ions. Unit cell contains 4 Na⁺ and 4 Cl⁻.

image.alt: NaCl crystal lattice showing alternating Na⁺ and Cl⁻
image.caption: NaCl structure: each ion surrounded by 6 oppositely charged ions in octahedral arrangement.

examples.explanation: Same structure as NaCl but with Mg²⁺ and O²⁻. Higher charges create stronger attraction, higher melting point (2852°C vs. 801°C for NaCl).

classifications.behavior: Adopted when cation is large relative to anion (radius ratio > 0.732); includes CsCl, CsBr, CsI.

examples.explanation: Cs⁺ at cube center, Cl⁻ at cube corners (or vice versa). Each ion has 8 nearest neighbors. Coordination number = 8. Larger Cs⁺ (167 pm) accommodates 8 Cl⁻ around it.

image.alt: CsCl crystal lattice showing 8:8 coordination
image.caption: CsCl structure: larger cation allows 8:8 coordination vs. 6:6 in NaCl.

classifications.value: Cubic lattice with 8:4 coordination for 1:2 ion ratio compounds
classifications.behavior: Adopted by compounds with formula MX₂ where cation is relatively large; includes CaF₂, SrF₂, BaCl₂.

examples.explanation: Ca²⁺ in face-centered cubic arrangement; F⁻ in all tetrahedral holes. Each Ca²⁺ has 8 F⁻ neighbors; each F⁻ has 4 Ca²⁺ neighbors.

image.alt: CaF₂ fluorite crystal structure
image.caption: Fluorite structure: 8:4 coordination for MX₂ compounds.

principles.statement: Lattice energy is the energy released when gaseous ions form one mole of solid ionic compound; it measures the strength of ionic bonding in the crystal.
principles.application: Explains melting point trends (higher lattice energy = higher mp), solubility (higher lattice energy = less soluble), and stability of ionic compounds.
principles.example: NaCl: 787 kJ/mol, mp 801°C. MgO: 3795 kJ/mol, mp 2852°C. Higher charges and smaller ions create higher lattice energy.

image.alt: Diagram showing lattice energy as energy released when ions form crystal
image.caption: Lattice energy: energy released when gaseous ions condense into solid crystal lattice.

principles.analogy: Like stacking magnets: organizing opposite poles together releases energy; more/bigger magnets (higher charges) release more energy.

principles.statement: The ratio of cation radius to anion radius (r₊/r₋) predicts the coordination number and crystal structure type of ionic compounds.
principles.example: NaCl: r_Na⁺/r_Cl⁻ = 102/181 = 0.56 → coordination 6 (NaCl structure). CsCl: r_Cs⁺/r_Cl⁻ = 167/181 = 0.92 → coordination 8 (CsCl structure).
principles.analogy: Like packing spheres: size ratio determines how many small spheres fit around a large sphere.

principles.statement: Ionic compounds must be electrically neutral: total positive charge from cations must equal total negative charge from anions.
principles.application: Determines compound formulas: Na⁺ + Cl⁻ → NaCl; Mg²⁺ + Cl⁻ → MgCl₂; Al³⁺ + O²⁻ → Al₂O₃.
principles.example: Al₂O₃: 2 Al³⁺ = +6 charge; 3 O²⁻ = -6 charge; net = 0 (neutral compound).
principles.analogy: Like balancing a checkbook: credits (positive charges) must equal debits (negative charges) for zero balance.

components.definition: Lattice energy depends on ion charges and sizes according to Coulomb's Law.
components.interpretation: Higher charges (q) increase lattice energy; smaller radii (r) increase lattice energy. Both factors make ionic bonds stronger.

worked_examples.title: Predicting Ionic Compound Formulas
worked_examples.problem: Predict the formulas of ionic compounds formed by: (a) calcium and chlorine, (b) aluminum and oxygen, (c) magnesium and nitrogen.

step_by_step.explanation: Determine ion charges from group numbers.
step_by_step.calculation: (a) Ca: Group 2 → Ca²⁺; Cl: Group 17 → Cl⁻; (b) Al: Group 13 → Al³⁺; O: Group 16 → O²⁻; (c) Mg: Group 2 → Mg²⁺; N: Group 15 → N³⁻

step_by_step.explanation: Balance charges to achieve electrical neutrality.
Write final formulas with lowest whole number ratios.
step_by_step.calculation: (a) CaCl₂; (b) Al₂O₃; (c) Mg₃N₂

step_by_step.explanation: Verify charge balance for each compound.

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) CaCl₂ (calcium chloride): one Ca²⁺ balances two Cl⁻. (b) Al₂O₃ (aluminum oxide): two Al³⁺ (+6) balance three O²⁻ (-6). (c) Mg₃N₂ (magnesium nitride): three Mg²⁺ (+6) balance two N³⁻ (-6). Ionic compound formulas are determined by balancing cation and anion charges to achieve electrical neutrality.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Charge balance determining ionic compound formulas

sections.introduction: Ionic compounds exhibit distinctive physical and chemical properties that directly result from their crystal lattice structures and strong electrostatic bonding. Understanding these properties enables prediction of compound behavior in various applications.

key_concept.definition: Ionic compounds are characterized by high melting and boiling points, brittleness, electrical conductivity when molten or dissolved, and solubility in polar solvents due to their crystal lattice structure and strong ionic bonding.
key_concept.context: These properties contrast sharply with covalent molecular compounds and reflect the strength and nature of ionic bonding in crystal lattices.

classifications.value: Ionic compounds require high temperatures to melt or boil due to strong lattice forces
characteristics: Strong electrostatic forces require significant energy to overcome
Lattice energy correlates with melting point
classifications.behavior: Melting requires breaking the crystal lattice; higher lattice energy = higher melting point; charge and size affect melting point.

examples.explanation: NaCl melts at 801°C. Strong attraction between Na⁺ and Cl⁻ requires significant thermal energy to break lattice. Compare to covalent H₂O (0°C mp).

image.alt: NaCl crystal melting at high temperature
image.caption: High melting point: strong ionic bonds require significant energy to break.

examples.explanation: MgO melts at 2852°C vs. NaCl at 801°C. Mg²⁺O²⁻ has higher charges than Na⁺Cl⁻, creating 4× stronger attraction (2×2 vs. 1×1).

classifications.value: Ionic compounds conduct electricity when molten or dissolved but not as solids
characteristics: Solid: ions fixed in lattice, no conductivity
Dissolved: ions mobile in solution, conducts electricity
classifications.behavior: Conductivity requires mobile charge carriers; solid ionic compounds have fixed ions; molten/dissolved have mobile ions.

examples.explanation: Solid NaCl: no conductivity (ions locked in lattice). Molten NaCl: conducts (ions free to move). NaCl(aq): conducts (ions dissociated in water).

image.alt: Conductivity test for solid, molten, and dissolved NaCl
image.caption: Ionic conductivity: only when ions are mobile (molten or dissolved).

examples.explanation: 2NaCl(l) → 2Na(l) + Cl₂(g). Electric current drives Na⁺ to cathode (reduction) and Cl⁻ to anode (oxidation), producing sodium metal and chlorine gas.

classifications.value: Ionic crystals are hard but brittle, shattering when struck rather than deforming
classifications.behavior: When struck, ions shift and like charges align, causing repulsion that cracks the crystal; no plastic deformation possible.

examples.explanation: When NaCl crystal is struck, layers shift. Na⁺ aligns with Na⁺ and Cl⁻ with Cl⁻, causing repulsion that cracks the crystal along cleavage planes.

image.alt: Diagram showing ionic crystal fracture when struck
image.caption: Brittleness: ion displacement causes like-charge repulsion, fracturing the crystal.

examples.explanation: Metals are malleable (delocalized electrons allow ion sliding); ionic compounds are brittle (fixed ion positions, like-charge repulsion on displacement).

classifications.value: Ionic compounds dissolve in polar solvents like water but not in nonpolar solvents
characteristics: Soluble in water and other polar solvents
Solubility varies with lattice energy and hydration energy
classifications.behavior: Water molecules surround and stabilize ions (hydration); hydration energy must overcome lattice energy for dissolution to occur.

examples.explanation: NaCl(s) → Na⁺(aq) + Cl⁻(aq). Water's partial negative O attracts Na⁺; partial positive H attracts Cl⁻. Ion-dipole forces overcome lattice energy.

image.alt: NaCl dissolving in water showing ion hydration
image.caption: Dissolution: water molecules surround ions, overcoming lattice energy through ion-dipole interactions.

examples.explanation: NaCl: highly soluble (36 g/100 mL). AgCl: insoluble (0.0002 g/100 mL). Higher lattice energy or lower hydration energy reduces solubility.

principles.statement: Higher lattice energy correlates with higher melting point, lower solubility, and greater hardness in ionic compounds.
principles.application: Predicts relative properties: MgO > NaCl in melting point and hardness due to higher charges; smaller ions increase lattice energy.
principles.example: MgO (3795 kJ/mol lattice energy): mp 2852°C, very hard. NaCl (787 kJ/mol): mp 801°C, moderately hard.
principles.analogy: Like glue strength: stronger glue (higher lattice energy) makes harder-to-separate materials (higher mp, lower solubility).

principles.statement: Ionic compounds dissolve when ion-dipole attractions between ions and solvent molecules overcome ion-ion attractions in the crystal lattice.
principles.application: Explains why ionic compounds dissolve in water (polar) but not hexane (nonpolar); why hydration energy must exceed lattice energy.
principles.example: NaCl in water: hydration energy (-784 kJ/mol) ≈ lattice energy (+787 kJ/mol), slightly endothermic but entropy-driven. NaCl in hexane: no ion-dipole forces, doesn't dissolve.

image.alt: Diagram showing ion-dipole interactions between ions and water molecules
image.caption: Ion-dipole interactions: water's partial charges attract and stabilize dissolved ions.

worked_examples.title: Explaining Ionic Compound Properties
worked_examples.problem: Explain why: (a) NaCl has a high melting point (801°C), (b) solid NaCl doesn't conduct electricity but molten NaCl does, (c) NaCl dissolves in water but not in oil.

step_by_step.explanation: Explain high melting point.
step_by_step.calculation: NaCl has strong electrostatic attraction between Na⁺ and Cl⁻ in crystal lattice; lattice energy = 787 kJ/mol; requires high temperature to overcome

step_by_step.explanation: Explain conductivity difference.
step_by_step.calculation: Solid NaCl: ions fixed in lattice, can't move → no conductivity. Molten NaCl: ions free to move → carry electric current → conducts

step_by_step.explanation: Explain solubility difference.
step_by_step.calculation: Water: polar, forms ion-dipole interactions with Na⁺ and Cl⁻ → dissolves. Oil: nonpolar, no ion-dipole interactions → doesn't dissolve

step_by_step.explanation: Connect all properties to ionic bonding.
step_by_step.calculation: All properties stem from strong ionic bonds in crystal lattice and requirement for mobile ions for conductivity

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) High mp: strong electrostatic attraction in lattice requires significant energy to break (lattice energy 787 kJ/mol). (b) Conductivity: solid has fixed ions (no conduction); molten has mobile ions (conducts). (c) Solubility: water is polar and forms ion-dipole interactions; oil is nonpolar with no ion-dipole capability. All properties reflect the nature of ionic bonding and crystal lattice structure.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Connecting ionic bonding to observable compound properties

sections.section_title: Writing Formulas and Names for Ionic Compounds
sections.introduction: Ionic compounds follow systematic naming and formula-writing conventions based on the identities and charges of the constituent ions. Mastering these conventions enables clear communication about ionic compounds.

key_concept.definition: Ionic compound formulas show the simplest whole-number ratio of cations to anions that achieves electrical neutrality; names identify the cation first, then the anion with appropriate suffixes.
key_concept.context: Systematic nomenclature (IUPAC) ensures unambiguous identification of compounds; common names persist for some well-known compounds.

classifications.value: Compounds containing two elements: metal cation + nonmetal anion
classifications.behavior: Name = cation name + anion root + -ide; formula balances charges to neutrality.

examples.explanation: Cation: Na⁺ = sodium; Anion: Cl⁻ = chloride. Name: sodium chloride. Formula: NaCl (1:1 ratio balances +1 and -1).

image.alt: NaCl naming convention
image.caption: Binary ionic naming: cation name + anion root + -ide.

examples.explanation: Cation: Mg²⁺ = magnesium; Anion: Cl⁻ = chloride. Name: magnesium chloride. Formula: MgCl₂ (1 Mg²⁺ balances 2 Cl⁻).
Cation: Al³⁺ = aluminum; Anion: O²⁻ = oxide. Name: aluminum oxide. Formula: Al₂O₃ (2 Al³⁺ = +6, 3 O²⁻ = -6, balanced).

classifications.value: Transition metals and some main-group metals form multiple cations requiring Roman numeral specification
classifications.behavior: Roman numeral specifies which cation charge is present; essential for metals like Fe, Cu, Sn, Pb that form multiple ions.

examples.explanation: FeCl₂: Fe²⁺ + 2Cl⁻ → iron(II) chloride. FeCl₃: Fe³⁺ + 3Cl⁻ → iron(III) chloride. Roman numeral distinguishes the two compounds.

image.alt: Iron(II) chloride vs. iron(III) chloride
image.caption: Variable-charge metals: Roman numerals specify cation charge.

examples.explanation: CuO: Cu²⁺ + O²⁻ → copper(II) oxide. Cu₂O: 2Cu⁺ + O²⁻ → copper(I) oxide. Different colors: CuO black, Cu₂O red.

classifications.value: Compounds containing polyatomic ions (charged groups of atoms)
characteristics: Polyatomic ion names must be memorized
Parentheses used when multiple polyatomic ions
classifications.behavior: Treat polyatomic ion as single unit; use parentheses if more than one needed; balance charges normally.

examples.explanation: Cation: Na⁺ = sodium; Anion: NO₃⁻ = nitrate. Name: sodium nitrate. Formula: NaNO₃ (1:1 ratio).

image.alt: Sodium nitrate structure
image.caption: Polyatomic ions: treat as single unit in naming and formula writing.

examples.explanation: Cation: Ca²⁺ = calcium; Anion: NO₃⁻ = nitrate. Need 2 NO₃⁻ to balance Ca²⁺. Name: calcium nitrate. Formula: Ca(NO₃)₂ (parentheses show 2 nitrate ions).
Cation: NH₄⁺ = ammonium; Anion: Cl⁻ = chloride. Name: ammonium chloride. Formula: NH₄Cl. Ammonium is polyatomic cation.

principles.statement: The total positive charge from cations must equal the total negative charge from anions in any ionic compound formula.
principles.application: Determines subscripts in formulas: cross charges to get subscripts, then reduce to lowest ratio.
principles.example: Al³⁺ and O²⁻: cross charges → Al₂O₃. Mg²⁺ and Cl⁻: cross charges → MgCl₂. Na⁺ and SO₄²⁻: cross charges → Na₂SO₄.
principles.analogy: Like a seesaw: total weight (charge) on each side must balance for equilibrium (neutrality).

principles.statement: Ionic compound formulas show the simplest whole-number ratio of ions, not the actual number in the crystal.
principles.application: Reduces formulas: Mg₂O₂ → MgO; Ca₂S₂ → CaS. The empirical formula represents the repeating unit.
principles.example: NaCl formula doesn't mean 1 Na⁺ and 1 Cl⁻ exist; it means 1:1 ratio throughout the crystal lattice.
principles.analogy: Like a recipe ratio: 1:1 flour:sugar doesn't mean 1 cup each, just that ratio is maintained regardless of batch size.

worked_examples.title: Writing Formulas from Names
worked_examples.problem: Write formulas for: (a) potassium sulfide, (b) iron(III) oxide, (c) ammonium phosphate.

step_by_step.explanation: Identify ions from compound names.
step_by_step.calculation: (a) K⁺ + S²⁻; (b) Fe³⁺ + O²⁻; (c) NH₄⁺ + PO₄³⁻

step_by_step.explanation: Balance charges to achieve neutrality.
Write formulas with parentheses where needed.
step_by_step.calculation: (a) K₂S (no parentheses needed); (b) Fe₂O₃ (no parentheses); (c) (NH₄)₃PO₄ (parentheses around NH₄⁺)

step_by_step.explanation: Verify charge balance for each formula.

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) K₂S (potassium sulfide): 2 K⁺ balance 1 S²⁻. (b) Fe₂O₃ (iron(III) oxide): 2 Fe³⁺ (+6) balance 3 O²⁻ (-6). (c) (NH₄)₃PO₄ (ammonium phosphate): 3 NH₄⁺ (+3) balance 1 PO₄³⁻ (-3). Parentheses used for polyatomic ions when more than one is needed.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Writing ionic compound formulas from names using charge balance

worked_examples.title: Naming Ionic Compounds from Formulas
worked_examples.problem: Name the following compounds: (a) CaBr₂, (b) CuSO₄, (c) (NH₄)₂CO₃.

step_by_step.explanation: Identify cation and anion in each formula.
step_by_step.calculation: (a) Ca²⁺ + Br⁻; (b) Cu²⁺ + SO₄²⁻; (c) NH₄⁺ + CO₃²⁻

step_by_step.explanation: Determine if cation has variable charge.
step_by_step.calculation: (a) Ca: Group 2, fixed +2 → no Roman numeral; (b) Cu: transition metal, variable → need Roman numeral; (c) NH₄⁺: polyatomic, fixed +1 → no Roman numeral

step_by_step.explanation: Name cations and anions.
step_by_step.calculation: (a) Ca²⁺ = calcium, Br⁻ = bromide; (b) Cu²⁺ = copper(II), SO₄²⁻ = sulfate; (c) NH₄⁺ = ammonium, CO₃²⁻ = carbonate

step_by_step.explanation: Combine names (cation first, anion second).
step_by_step.calculation: (a) calcium bromide; (b) copper(II) sulfate; (c) ammonium carbonate

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) CaBr₂: calcium bromide (Ca is Group 2, fixed charge). (b) CuSO₄: copper(II) sulfate (Cu is +2 to balance SO₄²⁻, needs Roman numeral). (c) (NH₄)₂CO₃: ammonium carbonate (both polyatomic ions, memorized names). Naming follows cation-first convention with Roman numerals for variable-charge metals.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Naming ionic compounds from formulas with appropriate conventions

summary: Ionic bonding occurs through complete electron transfer from metal atoms to nonmetal atoms, creating oppositely charged ions that attract electrostatically. Metals lose electrons to form cations (Na → Na⁺, Mg → Mg²⁺); nonmetals gain electrons to form anions (Cl → Cl⁻, O → O²⁻). Both achieve noble gas configurations (octet rule). Electrostatic attraction follows Coulomb's Law: F ∝ q₁q₂/r², where higher charges and smaller distances create stronger bonds. Ionic compounds form crystal lattices (NaCl: 6:6 coordination; CsCl: 8:8 coordination) with high melting points (strong lattice energy), brittleness (like-charge repulsion on displacement), electrical conductivity when molten/dissolved (mobile ions), and solubility in polar solvents (ion-dipole interactions). Lattice energy (energy released when gaseous ions form solid) correlates with melting point and hardness. Compound formulas balance charges to neutrality (Mg²⁺ + 2Cl⁻ → MgCl₂); names list cation first, anion second with -ide suffix for monatomic anions. Variable-charge metals require Roman numerals (Fe²⁺ = iron(II), Fe³⁺ = iron(III)). Polyatomic ions (NH₄⁺, SO₄²⁻, NO₃⁻) are treated as single units. Ionic bonding explains properties of salts, minerals, and many industrial compounds.
